skill.name: snowtower-user
skill.description: Guides SnowTower users through Snowflake access requests, RSA key setup, first connections, and common troubleshooting steps.
skill.summary: Guide end users through SnowTower Snowflake access, RSA key setup, connection testing, and basic troubleshooting.
skill.author: Database-Tycoon

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many findings, but most are false positives from Markdown code fences, example commands, and Snowflake key configuration text. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or malicious execution intent was found. The skill still carries medium risk because it instructs users to generate and manage private key files under ~/.ssh.

medium_findings.title: Private Key Handling Guidance
medium_findings.description: The guide instructs users to create a Snowflake RSA private key under ~/.ssh, set permissions, and reference that file from CLI and Python connection examples. This is legitimate Snowflake onboarding guidance, but mishandling the generated private key could expose account access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file clearly contains user-facing commands and examples involving private key files. The surrounding text also warns users not to share the private key, so the concern is operational risk rather than confirmed malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported Ruby backtick execution, but the flagged locations are Markdown fenced code blocks and examples. They document commands or SQL for users and are not executable skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-numbered review shows fenced Markdown examples, not Ruby code or runtime command execution. No file in the skill executes these blocks automatically.

low_findings.title: Relative GitHub Link Flagged as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The path traversal finding points to a Markdown link for an access request form using ../../issues/new/choose. It is a repository-relative GitHub issue URL, not filesystem traversal code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious sequence appears inside a Markdown hyperlink. There is no code path that opens local files or resolves the link against the filesystem.

low_findings.title: Environment Secret Detection False Positive
low_findings.description: The env_access findings point to Snowflake connector fields named private_key_file_pwd and private_key_file. The guide does not read environment variables or instruct users to expose secret environment values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are Python connector keyword names in documentation. No process environment access or secret harvesting behavior is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Run Shell Commands for Key Setup
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide includes openssl, chmod, cat, snow, and ls examples that users may run locally. These commands are expected for Snowflake RSA setup, but users should verify paths and avoid sharing private key contents.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly visible in bash code blocks. They are not automatically executed by the skill, but they affect local key material and connection setup.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive File Path References
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill repeatedly references ~/.ssh/snowflake_rsa_key.p8 and ~/.ssh/snowflake_rsa_key.pub. The private key path is sensitive, while the public key path is intended for access requests.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sensitive file paths are explicit and repeated. The content distinguishes private and public keys, reducing but not removing handling risk.

content.value_statement: Snowflake onboarding often fails when users miss key setup, request details, or first connection checks. This skill gives a clear SnowTower workflow for access requests, RSA keys, connection setup, and basic troubleshooting.
actual_capabilities: Explains the three-step SnowTower access request workflow.
Shows how to generate RSA key files for Snowflake authentication.
Identifies which key content is safe to submit in an access request.
Provides Snow CLI and Python connection examples for approved users.
Lists first-session Snowflake checks for roles, warehouses, and databases.
Troubleshoots common authentication, privilege, and connection issues.
limitations: Does not create Snowflake accounts or approve access requests.
Requires organization-specific account, role, warehouse, and URL details.
Does not validate that local RSA key files are stored securely.
Does not replace administrator review for additional permissions.

use_cases.title: Onboard a New Analyst
use_cases.description: Guide a new analyst through key generation, request submission, and first Snowflake connection checks.

use_cases.title: Prepare Programmatic Access
use_cases.description: Help an engineer configure RSA key authentication for Snow CLI or Python after approval.

use_cases.title: Resolve First Login Issues
use_cases.description: Walk a teammate through common authentication, role, warehouse, and VPN checks before escalating.

prompt_templates.title: Start My Access Request
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for first-time Snowflake access.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me request Snowflake access through SnowTower. I need the steps in order and a checklist of information to prepare.

prompt_templates.title: Check My RSA Key Setup
prompt_templates.scenario: User has generated keys and wants to confirm safe handling.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the SnowTower RSA key setup steps with me. Explain which file is private, which file is public, and what I should submit.

prompt_templates.title: Connect After Approval
prompt_templates.scenario: Approved user needs CLI or Python connection guidance.
prompt_templates.prompt: I have Snowflake approval, my username, account identifier, role, and warehouse. Help me connect with Snow CLI and Python.

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot Access Failure
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced troubleshooting across authentication and privileges.
prompt_templates.prompt: Diagnose my SnowTower Snowflake connection issue. Ask for the minimum safe details and help me isolate key, role, warehouse, and VPN problems.

output_examples.input: I need Snowflake access for the first time.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step onboarding checklist covering RSA key creation, public key submission, required request details, approval expectations, and first connection checks.

output_examples.input: My Snowflake authentication failed after approval.
output_examples.output: A focused troubleshooting path that checks account approval, key path, key permissions, submitted public key, account identifier, and network requirements.

output_examples.input: I need to know what access I get by default.
output_examples.output: A plain-language summary of the default role, personal development database, shared warehouse, and when to request additional permissions.

best_practices: Keep the private key local and share only the public key in access requests.
Confirm account, role, warehouse, and VPN details before troubleshooting code.
Request the smallest access scope that supports the business need.
anti_patterns: Do not paste private key contents into tickets, chats, prompts, or documents.
Do not request broad production access without a specific business reason.
Do not assume CLI access is approved before the SnowTower request is complete.

faq.question: Can this skill approve my Snowflake access?
faq.answer: No. It explains the request process, but an administrator must review and approve access.

faq.question: Which RSA key should I submit?
faq.answer: Submit only the public key. Keep the private key on your local machine and never share it.

faq.question: Does it support Snow CLI?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Snow CLI connection steps for users who already have approved account details.

faq.question: Does it support Python connections?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows a Snowflake connector example that references a local private key file.

faq.question: What if authentication fails?
faq.answer: Use the troubleshooting checklist for key permissions, submitted public key, account identifier, and network requirements.

faq.question: Is this safe for secrets?
faq.answer: It warns users not to share private keys, but users must still follow their organization security policies.
